voice_name: Laura — V7 plain explainer with brand examples

scenes.text: Growth is not one thing. Spotify, TikTok, and an Amazon store all grow, but by very different rules. First pick the engine.
There are three. Sticky protects a base. Viral builds a crowd. Paid buys customers profitably. Each has one test.
Sticky means retention, like Spotify. New customers pour in. Churn leaks out when people cancel. If twenty join and eight leave, the base rises. New must beat churn.

Sticky's real prize is MRR, monthly recurring revenue. Spotify charges ten dollars a month per user. A million users means ten million MRR. ARR is MRR times twelve. Churn one, and twelve months of revenue disappear.
Viral means users bring users, like TikTok. K is the average number of new users each user creates. Above one, branches multiply. Below one, the chain fades.
But why build an audience? Because some businesses sell access to the crowd. Amazon needs buyers before sellers care. YouTube needs attention before sponsors pay. The audience is the product.

Paid is a money machine. CAC is the cost to win one customer. Spend forty in ads, get one Shopify customer. LTV is what that customer pays back over time. Thirty a month for four months is one twenty. Forty in, one twenty out, scale it. But if it costs ninety to win a customer who only pays sixty, every new sale deepens the loss.
The wrong engine creates traps. Ads cannot patch a leaky subscription. Buying isolated users does not make a network. And going viral cannot fix a product that loses money on every sale.
Choose by asking one thing. Do customers repeat, like Spotify? Sticky. Do users make the network valuable, like TikTok? Viral. Do you buy customers one at a time, like Shopify ads? Paid.
Pick the engine your business needs. Then make its equation true.
